cards.front: What are the 6 steps of feedback amplifier analysis?
cards.back: 1) Identify topology. 2) Calculate β. 3) Draw open-loop circuit with loading. 4) Calculate A. 5) A_f = A/(1+Aβ). 6) R_if and R_of from topology rules.

cards.front: PNP emitter follower: what is β and why?
cards.back: β = 1. The full output voltage V_E is fed back to the emitter in series with V_BE. 100% feedback → maximum feedback effect.

cards.front: PNP emitter follower results: A_V, D, A_Vf, R_if, R_of
cards.back: A_V = 106.8, D = 107.8, A_Vf ≈ 0.99 ≈ 1, R_if = 118.6 kΩ, R_of = 1.93 Ω. Unity gain buffer with high R_in and low R_out.

cards.front: Two-stage CE amplifier: why is gain positive despite two CE stages?
cards.back: Each CE stage inverts: (-10) × (-10) = +100. Two inversions give non-inverting gain, allowing feedback to the emitter to correctly subtract and create negative feedback.

cards.front: Two-stage CE amplifier results: A_V1, A_V2, A_V, β, A_Vf
When can you use A_f ≈ 1/β instead of the exact formula?
cards.back: Only when Aβ >> 1 (typically Aβ > 10). If Aβ is modest (like 1.79), use the exact formula A_f = A/(1+Aβ). The approximation gives large error otherwise.

cards.front: What loading effects must be included in the open-loop model?
cards.back: The feedback network physically connects to both input and output. Its impedances load: (1) R_f in parallel with R_C2 at output. (2) R_E1 as emitter degeneration at input. Both must be included in calculating A.

cards.front: Why does 1/β = 100 overestimate A_Vf for the two-stage CE (where true A_Vf ≈ 64)?
cards.back: The approximation 1/β requires Aβ >> 1. Here Aβ ≈ 1.79, so D ≈ 2.81 (not ≈ Aβ). A/D = 181/2.81 ≈ 64, not 1/β = 100.

cards.front: To get β = 0.01 in the two-stage CE, what resistors are used and how?
cards.back: R_f = 47 kΩ, R_E1 = 470 Ω. β = R_E1/(R_f + R_E1) = 470/47470 ≈ 0.0099. The feedback voltage across R_E1 is a fraction (β) of the output voltage.

cards.front: For the two-stage CE (Series-Shunt), what are R_if and R_of?
cards.back: R_if = R_i × D (series mixing → increased). R_of = R_o / D (shunt sampling → decreased). With D = 2.81: R_if ≈ 70 kΩ, R_of ≈ 1.68 kΩ.

cards.front: Physical interpretation: why is R_of of the emitter follower ≈ 2 Ω?
cards.back: D = 107.8 and R_o ≈ 208 Ω. With 100% feedback (β = 1), the amplifier almost perfectly corrects any output voltage drop — from the load's perspective, it sees an extremely stiff source with R_of = 208/107.8 ≈ 2 Ω.

cards.front: How to calculate the open-loop output impedance R_o for the emitter follower?
cards.back: Look into the emitter with base driven through R_S. R_o = R_E || (h_ie + R_S)/(h_fe + 1). For R_S = 10k: = 4700 || (11100/51) = 4700 || 217.6 ≈ 208 Ω.
